questions.question_text: A broker receives an earnest money deposit on Friday afternoon. By when must the funds be deposited into the trust account?
distractors: By the next calendar day
Within 5 business days of receipt
Before the end of the following week
questions.explanation: Nevada requires trust funds to be deposited within 3 business days of receipt. Weekends and holidays do not count as business days. This timeline ensures client funds are promptly secured and properly accounted for in the trust account.

questions.question_text: A broker maintains $500 of personal funds in their trust account to cover bank fees. Does this comply with Nevada regulations?
questions.correct_answer: No, only up to $200 is permitted
distractors: Yes, any amount for maintenance is allowed
No, personal funds are never permitted
Yes, if the bank requires a minimum balance
questions.explanation: Nevada prohibits commingling personal and client funds in a trust account, with one exception: a broker may keep up to $200 of personal funds to maintain the account. Keeping $500 exceeds this limit and constitutes commingling. Bank minimum balance requirements do not override this rule.

questions.question_text: Where must a Nevada broker's trust account be established?
questions.correct_answer: At a Nevada bank or financial institution
distractors: At any FDIC-insured bank nationwide
At the broker's primary business bank
At any institution approved by the Division
questions.explanation: Nevada requires trust accounts to be maintained at a Nevada bank or financial institution. An out-of-state bank, even if FDIC-insured, does not satisfy this requirement. The account must also include 'trust account' in its name.

questions.question_text: A broker wants to place trust funds in an interest-bearing account and retain the interest as a service fee. Is this permitted?
distractors: Brokers may always retain trust account interest
Interest-bearing trust accounts are prohibited
Interest automatically belongs to the broker
questions.explanation: Nevada allows trust funds to be placed in interest-bearing accounts, but proper consent from the parties must be obtained. The broker cannot unilaterally decide to retain interest as a service fee. The disposition of interest must be agreed upon by the parties involved.

questions.question_text: A broker opens a trust account titled 'ABC Realty Operating Account' at a local Nevada bank. What is wrong with this account?
distractors: The account should be at a national bank
Operating accounts cannot hold trust funds
The broker's personal name must be listed
questions.explanation: Nevada requires the trust account name to include the words 'trust account.' Naming it an 'operating account' fails this requirement and could lead to confusion between business operating funds and client trust funds. The account must clearly identify its purpose.

questions.question_text: A salesperson collects a deposit check from a buyer at a showing on Saturday. What is the salesperson's immediate obligation?
questions.correct_answer: Promptly deliver the funds to the broker
distractors: Deposit the check into their personal account
Hold the check until the next business day
Deliver the check directly to the seller
questions.explanation: Nevada licensees must promptly deliver all client monies to their broker. The salesperson cannot hold funds, deposit them personally, or deliver them directly to the other party. The broker then has the responsibility to deposit the funds into the trust account within the required timeline.

questions.question_text: An investigator from the Real Estate Division arrives unannounced at a brokerage and requests to review trust account records. Must the broker comply?
questions.correct_answer: Yes, the Division may audit at any time
distractors: No, a written notice is required first
Only with a court order or subpoena
Yes, but only during scheduled office hours
questions.explanation: The Nevada Real Estate Division has the authority to audit trust accounts at any time without prior notice. Brokers must maintain accurate trust account records and make them available upon request. No court order, subpoena, or advance notice is required for a Division audit.

questions.question_text: Which record-keeping obligation applies to Nevada brokers regarding trust accounts?
distractors: File monthly statements with the Division
Have records audited by a CPA annually
Submit quarterly trust account balances online
questions.explanation: Nevada requires brokers to maintain accurate trust account records at all times. While the Division may audit at any time, brokers are not required to file monthly statements, obtain annual CPA audits, or submit quarterly balances. The obligation is to keep records accurate and available for review.

questions.question_text: A broker discovers that a salesperson deposited a client's earnest money into the brokerage operating account instead of the trust account. What has occurred?
questions.correct_answer: Commingling of client and business funds
distractors: A minor bookkeeping error with no penalty
An acceptable practice if corrected promptly
A violation only if the funds are spent
questions.explanation: Depositing client funds into the brokerage operating account constitutes commingling, which is prohibited under Nevada law. This violation occurs at the moment of improper deposit regardless of whether the funds are later moved or spent. It is not merely a bookkeeping error and carries potential disciplinary consequences.

questions.question_text: A buyer's agent receives three separate earnest money checks from different transactions in one day. How must the agent handle these funds?
questions.correct_answer: Deliver all checks promptly to the broker
distractors: Deposit each into separate personal accounts
Hold them until each transaction is ratified
Deliver them directly to each listing agent
questions.explanation: All client monies must be promptly delivered to the broker regardless of the number of transactions. The broker is then responsible for proper trust account deposits within 3 business days. Licensees cannot hold, personally deposit, or redirect client funds to other parties.
